chunk_summary: Guidelines for malnutrition triage and urgent referral in clinics addressing climate-related food insecurity, focusing on anthropometric thresholds, danger signs, and management strategies for severe and moderate acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months.
learning_objectives: Understand the anthropometric thresholds for diagnosing severe and moderate acute malnutrition (SAM and MAM).
Identify danger signs and complications that necessitate urgent referral to a nutrition rehabilitation center (NRC).
Learn outpatient management strategies for children with uncomplicated malnutrition.
Recognize the importance of linking families to local nutrition and food support systems.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined as MUAC <115 mm, weight-for-height/length z score < -3, or bilateral pitting edema.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is defined as MUAC 115–124 mm or z score between -3 and -2.
Urgent referral to an NRC is required for SAM with complications such as inability to feed, lethargy, severe dehydration, or severe anemia.
Outpatient management for uncomplicated malnutrition includes increased meal frequency, energy-dense foods, continued breastfeeding, and early follow-up within 7–14 days.
Dehydration in alert children should be treated with ORS, and unproven appetite tonics should be avoided.
reference_quote: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is present with MUAC <115 mm, weight-for-height/length z score < -3, or bilateral edema.
document_key_topics: Linking families to nutrition support systems
